title: Visualizing in R
text:
This section collects my notes and exercises on data visualization in R.

title: Position adjustment
text:
library(tidyverse)
library(ggthemes)

title: Position adjustment
text:
Miscellaneous
Comparing geom_jitter with geom_count:

ggplot(
  data = mpg, 
  mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)
) + 
  geom_count(position = "jitter")








Default position for geom_boxplot:

# Right
ggplot(
  data = mpg,
  mapping = aes(x = class, y = hwy)) +
  geom_boxplot()







# Left
ggplot(data = mpg,
       mapping = aes(x = class, y = hwy)) +
  geom_boxplot(position = "dodge2")

title: Exploratory data analysis - EDA
text:
Generate questions about the data
Search for answers by visualizing, transforming, and modelling the data
Use learned to refine question and/or generate new question







Note



To do data cleaning, you’ll need to deploy all the tools of EDA: visualization, transformation, and modelling.

title: Exploratory data analysis - EDA
text:
Generate questions about the data
Search for answers by visualizing, transforming, and modelling the data
Use learned to refine question and/or generate new question







Note



To do data cleaning, you’ll need to deploy all the tools of EDA: visualization, transformation, and modelling.

title: Exploratory data analysis - EDA
text:
Prerequisites

library(tidyverse)
library(ggthemes)

Goal: Develope understanding of the data by asking questions.
Key: To ask quality question one must first generate a large quantity of questions






Important



Two types of questions will always be useful for making discoveries within the data:

What type of variation occurs within my variables?
What type of covariation occurs between my variables?

title: Exploratory data analysis - EDA
text:
Variation
Variations is the tendency of the values of a variable to change from measurement to measurement. Each of measurement will include small amount of error that varies from measurement to measurement. Variables can also vary across different subjects or at different times. Every variable has own pattern of variation. Best way to understand that pattern is to visualize the distribution of the variable’s values

Diamond dataset

# ?diamonds
# str(diamonds)
diamonds
glimpse(diamonds)

Start by visualizing distribution of weights (carat) of ~54,000 diamonds from the diamonds dataset. caratis a numerical vairable, can use histogram:

ggplot(
  data = diamonds,
  mapping = aes(x = carat)
) + 
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.5)










Typical values
In both, bar charts and histograms, tall bars show common values of a variable, and shorter bars show less-common values. Places with no bar, indicate that these values were not seen in the data. Look for anything unexpected:

Which values are the most common? Why?
Which values are rare? Why? Does this match our expectation?
Can we see any unusual pattern? What might explain them?

Distribution of carat for smaller diamonds:

smaller <- diamonds |>
  filter(carat < 3) |>
  ggplot(mapping = aes(x = carat)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.01)

print(smaller)








Questions:

Why are there more diamonds at whole carats and common fractions of carats?
Why are there more diamonds slightly to the right of each peak than there are slightly to the left of each peak?

Visualizations can also reveal clusters, which suggest existence of subgroups:

How are the observations within each subgroup similar to each other?
How are the observations in separate clusters different from each other?
How can you explain or describe the clusters?
Why might the appearance of clusters be misleading?



Unusual values
Outliers are observations that are unusual; Data points not fitting the pattern. Sometimes just data entry error, sometimes extreme values observed in data collection, sometimes they suggest important new findings
Sometimes, outliers are not easy to spot. E.g. take the distribution of the y variable from the diamonds dataset. The only evidence of outliers is the unusually wide limits on the x-axis.:

ggplot(
  data = diamonds, 
  mapping = aes(x = y)
) + 
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.5)








Rare bins are very short, making it very difficult to see them. We need to zoom to small values of the y-axis with coord_cartesian():

ggplot(
  data = diamonds,
  mapping = aes(x = y)
) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 50))














Note



coord_cartesian() also has an xlim() argument for when you need to zoom into the x-axis. ggplot2 also has xlim() and ylim() functions that work slightly differently: they throw away the data outside the limits.


Can see and filter for the unusual items:

unusual <- diamonds |>
  filter(y < 3 | y > 20) |>
  select(price, x, y, z) |>
  arrange(y)

print(unusual)

# A tibble: 9 × 4
  price     x     y     z
  <int> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1  5139  0      0    0   
2  6381  0      0    0   
3 12800  0      0    0   
4 15686  0      0    0   
5 18034  0      0    0   
6  2130  0      0    0   
7  2130  0      0    0   
8  2075  5.15  31.8  5.12
9 12210  8.09  58.9  8.06








Note



The y variable measures one of the three dimensions of these diamonds, in mm. We know that diamonds can’t have a width of 0mm, so these values must be incorrect. By doing EDA, we have discovered missing data that was coded as 0, which we never would have found by simply searching for NAs. Going forward we might choose to re-code these values as NAs in order to prevent misleading calculations. We might also suspect that measurements of 32mm and 59mm are implausible: those diamonds are over an inch long, but don’t cost hundreds of thousands of dollars!




Unusual values
Two options when encountering unusual values in the dataset:

Drop entire row with strange values (not recommended):


diamonds2 <- diamonds |> 
  filter(between(y, 3, 20)) # between() as shortcut for x >= left & <= right; see ?between







Tip



?filter: These functions are used to subset a data frame, applying the expressions in … to determine which rows should be kept (for filter()) or dropped ( for filter_out()).



Replace unusual values with missing values (recommended): ::: {.callout-note} Easiest way is by mutate() to replace variable with modified copy. Can use ìf_else() function to replace values with NA: :::


diamonds2 <- diamonds |>
  mutate(y = ifelse(y < 3 | y > 20, NA, y))

Now, when plotting, ggplot will not include NA’s, but will warn!

ggplot(
  data = diamonds2,
  mapping = aes(x = x, y = y)
) + 
  geom_point(na.rm = TRUE) # supressing the warning










What makes observations with missing values different?
Example: nycflights13::flights, missing values in the dep_time variable indicate that the flight was cancelled. Want to compare scheduled departure times for cancelled and non-cancelled times. Can do this by making new variable, using ìs.na() to check if dep_timeis missing:

cancelled_flights <- nycflights13::flights |>
  mutate(
    cancelled = is.na(dep_time),
    sched_hour = sched_dep_time %/% 100, 
    sched_min = sched_dep_time %% 100,
    sched_dep_time = sched_hour + (sched_min / 60)
    )

ggplot(
  data = cancelled_flights,
  mapping = aes(x = sched_dep_time)) +
  geom_freqpoly(aes(color = cancelled), binwidth = 1/4)







# Alternatively, can facet by cancelled flights, and set scales free

ggplot(
  data = cancelled_flights,
  mapping = aes(x = sched_dep_time)
) +
  geom_freqpoly(mapping = aes(color = cancelled), binwidth = 1/4) +
  facet_wrap(~cancelled, scale = "free") +
  scale_color_colorblind()







# Or, even better, see upcoming content, use after_stat()

ggplot(
  data = cancelled_flights,
  mapping = aes(x = sched_dep_time, y = after_stat(density))
) +
  geom_freqpoly(mapping = aes(color = cancelled), binwidth = 1/4)














Note



geom_freqpoly {ggplot2}: Visualise the distribution of a single continuous variable by dividing the x axis into bins and counting the number of observations in each bin. Histograms (geom_histogram()) display the counts with bars; frequency polygons (geom_freqpoly()) display the counts with lines.

title: Exploratory data analysis - EDA
text:
Covariation
Variation describing behavior within a variable; Covariation describing behavior between variables.
Covariation is the tendency for the values of two or more variables to vary together in a related way. Best way to spot covariation is by visualizing the relationship between two or more variables.

A categorical & numerical variable
Example: Comparing price of diamond vs. quality (cut) using geom_freqpoly():

# Left
ggplot(
  data = diamonds, 
  mapping = aes(x = price) 
) +
  geom_freqpoly(mapping = aes(color = cut), binwidth = 500, linewidth = 0.75) +   scale_color_colorblind()







# Right
ggplot(
  data = diamonds, 
  mapping = aes(x = price, y = after_stat(density)) 
# standardized now, instead of displaying count only, making comparison easier
# See ?geom_freqploy or ?stat_bin, the underlying mechanism of geom_freqpol()
) +
  geom_freqpoly(mapping = aes(color = cut), binwidth = 500, linewidth = 0.75) +   scale_color_colorblind()







# Simpler approach is side-by-side boxplots
ggplot(
  data = diamonds,
  mapping = aes(x = cut, y = price)) +
    geom_boxplot()














Tip



cutis ordered factor, BUT many categorcial variables dont have such intrinsic order, hence reordering is appropriate. Do so by fct_reorder()



# Example 1
ggplot(
  data = diamonds,
  mapping = aes(x = cut, y = price)
) + 
 geom_boxplot(
   mapping = aes(x = fct_reorder(cut, price, median)))







# Example 2
ggplot(
  data = mpg, 
  mapping = aes(x = fct_reorder(class, hwy, median), y = hwy)
) +
  geom_boxplot()







# Example 3: Flipping axis with long names
ggplot(
  data = mpg, 
  mapping = aes(y = fct_reorder(class, hwy, median), x = hwy)
) +
  geom_boxplot()







# Example 4: use coord_flip()
ggplot(
  data = mpg,
  mapping = aes(x = fct_reorder(class, hwy, median), y = hwy)
) + 
  geom_boxplot() +
  coord_flip()










Miscellaneous






Note



One problem with boxplots is that they were developed in an era of much smaller datasets and tend to display a prohibitively large number of “outlying values”. One approach to remedy this problem is the letter value plot.



# Example of lvplot, refer to ?lvplot
# install.packages("lvplot")
library(lvplot)
?lvplot

ggplot(
  data = diamonds,
  mapping = aes(x = cut, y = price)
) + 
  geom_lv(mapping = aes(fill = after_stat(LV))) +
  scale_y_sqrt() +
  theme_bw()








There is also the option to display a relationship via geom_violin().

ggplot(
  data = diamonds,
  mapping = aes(x = fct_reorder(cut, price, median), y = price)
) + 
  geom_violin(quantile.linetype = 1L, quantile.color = "red")










Two categorical variables
To visualize covariation between two categorical variables, need to count the number of observations forch each combination of levels of these categorical variables.

# One way is via geom_count()
ggplot(
  data = diamonds,
  mapping = aes(x = cut, y = color)
) + 
  geom_count()














Note



Size of circles display how many observations occurred at each combination of values. Covariation will appear as a strong correlation.


Another approach:

diamonds |> 
  count(color, cut) |>
  ggplot(
    mapping = aes(x = color, y = cut)
  ) +
  geom_tile(
    mapping = aes(fill = n)
  )







# Alternatively: Distribution of cut within color
diamonds |> 
  count(color, cut) |>
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n), .by = color) |>
  ggplot(
    mapping = aes(x = color, y = cut)
  ) +
  geom_tile(
    mapping = aes(fill = prop)
  )







# Alternatively: Distribution of color within cut
diamonds |> 
  count(color, cut) |>
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n), .by = cut) |>
  ggplot(
    mapping = aes(x = color, y = cut)
  ) +
  geom_tile(
    mapping = aes(fill = prop)
  )














Tip



For unordered categorical variables use seriation package, for larger plots use heatmaply package.




Two numerical variables
Great way to visualize the covariation between two numerical variables: draw a scatterplot with geom_point().

ggplot(
  data = diamonds,
  mapping = aes(x = carat, y = price)
) +
  geom_point()







# Focusing on bulk of diamonds smaller than 3 carat
diamonds |> 
  filter(carat < 3) |>
  ggplot(
    mapping = aes(x = carat, y = price)
  ) + 
  geom_point()














Warning



Scatterplots become less useful when dataset grows, as points begin to overplot; can fix problem with alpha argument.



smaller <- diamonds |>
  filter(carat < 3)
# Inducing transparency
ggplot(
  data = smaller,
  mapping = aes(x = carat, y = price)
) +
  geom_point(alpha = 1/100)








Alternatively, can use bins, i.e. geom_bin2d() and geom_hex(), to bin in two dimensions. Both divide coordinate plane into 2d bins and use fill color to display how many points fall into each bin.

geom_bin2d() creates rectangular bins.
geom_hex() creates hexagonal bins.


# Install package for geom_hex()
# install.packages("hexbin")

# Example geom_bin2d()
ggplot(
  data = smaller,
  mapping = aes(x = carat, y = price)
) + 
  geom_bin2d()







# Example geom_hex()
ggplot(
  data = smaller,
  mapping = aes(x = carat, y = price)
) +
  geom_hex()








Alternatively, bin one continuous variable, acting as categorical variable:

ggplot(
  data = smaller,
  mapping = aes(x = carat, y = price)
) + 
  geom_boxplot(
    mapping = aes(group = cut_width(carat, 0.1)), varwidth = TRUE
  )

Warning: Orientation is not uniquely specified when both the x and y aesthetics are
continuous. Picking default orientation 'x'.















Note



cut_with(x, width) divides x into bins of width width. varwidth = TRUE makes the width of boxplot proportional to number of points.




Miscellaneous
Instead summarizing conditional distribution with boxplots, could use frequency polygon:

ggplot(
  data = smaller,
  mapping = aes(x = price, color = cut_number(carat, 6))
) +
  geom_freqpoly()

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.








ggplot(
  data = smaller,
  mapping = aes(x = price, color = cut_width(carat, 0.2))
) +
  geom_freqpoly()

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.

title: Statistical Transformation
text:
library(tidyverse)
library(ggthemes)

title: Statistical Transformation
text:
Miscellaneous
Difference between geom_bar() and geom_col():

ggplot(diamonds, aes(x = cut)) +
  geom_bar() # here we use default stat_count()







ggplot(diamonds, mapping = aes(x = cut, y = depth)) + 
  geom_col() # here geom_bar() stacks the "depth" variable







diamonds |> 
  summarize(mean_depth = mean(depth, na.rm = TRUE), .by = cut) |>
  ggplot(mapping = aes(x = cut, y = mean_depth)) + 
  geom_col() # here we compute the average depth and then plot using geom_col()














Important



Documentation: geom_bar() makes the height of the bar proportional to the number of cases in each group. If you want the heights of the bars to represent values in the data, use geom_col() instead. geom_bar() uses stat_count() by default: it counts the number of cases at each x position. geom_col() uses stat_identity(): it leaves the data as is.

title: facets
text:
Splitting plots into subplots, each displaying one subset of the data.






Note



Use categorical variable!





library(tidyverse)
library(ggthemes)





mpg

# A tibble: 234 × 11
   manufacturer model      displ  year   cyl trans drv     cty   hwy fl    class
   <chr>        <chr>      <dbl> <int> <int> <chr> <chr> <int> <int> <chr> <chr>
 1 audi         a4           1.8  1999     4 auto… f        18    29 p     comp…
 2 audi         a4           1.8  1999     4 manu… f        21    29 p     comp…
 3 audi         a4           2    2008     4 manu… f        20    31 p     comp…
 4 audi         a4           2    2008     4 auto… f        21    30 p     comp…
 5 audi         a4           2.8  1999     6 auto… f        16    26 p     comp…
 6 audi         a4           2.8  1999     6 manu… f        18    26 p     comp…
 7 audi         a4           3.1  2008     6 auto… f        18    27 p     comp…
 8 audi         a4 quattro   1.8  1999     4 manu… 4        18    26 p     comp…
 9 audi         a4 quattro   1.8  1999     4 auto… 4        16    25 p     comp…
10 audi         a4 quattro   2    2008     4 manu… 4        20    28 p     comp…
# ℹ 224 more rows

glimpse(mpg)

Rows: 234
Columns: 11
$ manufacturer <chr> "audi", "audi", "audi", "audi", "audi", "audi", "audi", "…
$ model        <chr> "a4", "a4", "a4", "a4", "a4", "a4", "a4", "a4 quattro", "…
$ displ        <dbl> 1.8, 1.8, 2.0, 2.0, 2.8, 2.8, 3.1, 1.8, 1.8, 2.0, 2.0, 2.…
$ year         <int> 1999, 1999, 2008, 2008, 1999, 1999, 2008, 1999, 1999, 200…
$ cyl          <int> 4, 4, 4, 4, 6, 6, 6, 4, 4, 4, 4, 6, 6, 6, 6, 6, 6, 8, 8, …
$ trans        <chr> "auto(l5)", "manual(m5)", "manual(m6)", "auto(av)", "auto…
$ drv          <chr> "f", "f", "f", "f", "f", "f", "f", "4", "4", "4", "4", "4…
$ cty          <int> 18, 21, 20, 21, 16, 18, 18, 18, 16, 20, 19, 15, 17, 17, 1…
$ hwy          <int> 29, 29, 31, 30, 26, 26, 27, 26, 25, 28, 27, 25, 25, 25, 2…
$ fl           <chr> "p", "p", "p", "p", "p", "p", "p", "p", "p", "p", "p", "p…
$ class        <chr> "compact", "compact", "compact", "compact", "compact", "c…

# ?mpg, to get access to variables used in ds
# str(mpg), to access structure of object





ggplot(
  data = mpg,
  mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy) ) + 
  geom_point() + 
  facet_wrap(~cyl)












ggplot(data = mpg,
       mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  geom_point() + 
  facet_grid(drv ~ cyl) # double sided formula: rows ~ cols







ggplot(data = mpg, 
       mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy) ) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(color = class)) + 
  scale_color_colorblind() + # by default, each facet shares same scale
  # set scales = "free", to allow different scales for both axis # other option: "free_x", "free_y" 
  facet_grid(drv ~ cyl, scales = "free")














ggplot(
  data = mpg,
  mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  geom_point() + 
  facet_wrap(~displ)  








A: Creates facet for every unique value of displ.




ggplot(
  data = mpg,
  mapping = aes(x = drv, y = cyl)) + 
  geom_point() + # compare to: ggplot( data = mpg, mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy))
  geom_point() + facet_grid(drv ~ cyl) # 








A: No corresponding values for some combinations.




ggplot( data = mpg, mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy) ) + geom_point() + facet_grid(drv ~ .)







ggplot( data = mpg ) + geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + facet_grid(. ~ cyl)








A: See ?facet_grid,‘. dot in the formula used to indicate there should be no faceting on this dimension (either row or column)’




#|label: Q4

ggplot(data = mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  facet_wrap(~cyl, nrow = 2)








A: Faceting challenging with large data set; Distinguish characteristics through colors might work better.




ggplot(
  data = mpg,
  mapping = aes(x = displ) ) + 
  geom_histogram() + 
  facet_grid(drv ~.) # => best representation of engine size, when faceting rowwise 

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.








ggplot(data = mpg,
       mapping = aes(x = displ) ) + 
  geom_histogram() + 
  facet_grid(.~ drv)

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.








# Note: previous plot equivalent to see below 
ggplot(data = mpg,
       mapping = aes(x = displ) ) + 
  geom_histogram() + 
  facet_wrap(~drv)

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.













ggplot(data = mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  facet_grid(drv ~ .)







ggplot(data = mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  facet_wrap(~drv, nrow = 3)

title: facets
text:
Splitting plots into subplots, each displaying one subset of the data.






Note



Use categorical variable!





library(tidyverse)
library(ggthemes)





mpg

# A tibble: 234 × 11
   manufacturer model      displ  year   cyl trans drv     cty   hwy fl    class
   <chr>        <chr>      <dbl> <int> <int> <chr> <chr> <int> <int> <chr> <chr>
 1 audi         a4           1.8  1999     4 auto… f        18    29 p     comp…
 2 audi         a4           1.8  1999     4 manu… f        21    29 p     comp…
 3 audi         a4           2    2008     4 manu… f        20    31 p     comp…
 4 audi         a4           2    2008     4 auto… f        21    30 p     comp…
 5 audi         a4           2.8  1999     6 auto… f        16    26 p     comp…
 6 audi         a4           2.8  1999     6 manu… f        18    26 p     comp…
 7 audi         a4           3.1  2008     6 auto… f        18    27 p     comp…
 8 audi         a4 quattro   1.8  1999     4 manu… 4        18    26 p     comp…
 9 audi         a4 quattro   1.8  1999     4 auto… 4        16    25 p     comp…
10 audi         a4 quattro   2    2008     4 manu… 4        20    28 p     comp…
# ℹ 224 more rows

glimpse(mpg)

Rows: 234
Columns: 11
$ manufacturer <chr> "audi", "audi", "audi", "audi", "audi", "audi", "audi", "…
$ model        <chr> "a4", "a4", "a4", "a4", "a4", "a4", "a4", "a4 quattro", "…
$ displ        <dbl> 1.8, 1.8, 2.0, 2.0, 2.8, 2.8, 3.1, 1.8, 1.8, 2.0, 2.0, 2.…
$ year         <int> 1999, 1999, 2008, 2008, 1999, 1999, 2008, 1999, 1999, 200…
$ cyl          <int> 4, 4, 4, 4, 6, 6, 6, 4, 4, 4, 4, 6, 6, 6, 6, 6, 6, 8, 8, …
$ trans        <chr> "auto(l5)", "manual(m5)", "manual(m6)", "auto(av)", "auto…
$ drv          <chr> "f", "f", "f", "f", "f", "f", "f", "4", "4", "4", "4", "4…
$ cty          <int> 18, 21, 20, 21, 16, 18, 18, 18, 16, 20, 19, 15, 17, 17, 1…
$ hwy          <int> 29, 29, 31, 30, 26, 26, 27, 26, 25, 28, 27, 25, 25, 25, 2…
$ fl           <chr> "p", "p", "p", "p", "p", "p", "p", "p", "p", "p", "p", "p…
$ class        <chr> "compact", "compact", "compact", "compact", "compact", "c…

# ?mpg, to get access to variables used in ds
# str(mpg), to access structure of object





ggplot(
  data = mpg,
  mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy) ) + 
  geom_point() + 
  facet_wrap(~cyl)












ggplot(data = mpg,
       mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  geom_point() + 
  facet_grid(drv ~ cyl) # double sided formula: rows ~ cols







ggplot(data = mpg, 
       mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy) ) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(color = class)) + 
  scale_color_colorblind() + # by default, each facet shares same scale
  # set scales = "free", to allow different scales for both axis # other option: "free_x", "free_y" 
  facet_grid(drv ~ cyl, scales = "free")














ggplot(
  data = mpg,
  mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  geom_point() + 
  facet_wrap(~displ)  








A: Creates facet for every unique value of displ.




ggplot(
  data = mpg,
  mapping = aes(x = drv, y = cyl)) + 
  geom_point() + # compare to: ggplot( data = mpg, mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy))
  geom_point() + facet_grid(drv ~ cyl) # 








A: No corresponding values for some combinations.




ggplot( data = mpg, mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy) ) + geom_point() + facet_grid(drv ~ .)







ggplot( data = mpg ) + geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + facet_grid(. ~ cyl)








A: See ?facet_grid,‘. dot in the formula used to indicate there should be no faceting on this dimension (either row or column)’




#|label: Q4

ggplot(data = mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  facet_wrap(~cyl, nrow = 2)








A: Faceting challenging with large data set; Distinguish characteristics through colors might work better.




ggplot(
  data = mpg,
  mapping = aes(x = displ) ) + 
  geom_histogram() + 
  facet_grid(drv ~.) # => best representation of engine size, when faceting rowwise 

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.








ggplot(data = mpg,
       mapping = aes(x = displ) ) + 
  geom_histogram() + 
  facet_grid(.~ drv)

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.








# Note: previous plot equivalent to see below 
ggplot(data = mpg,
       mapping = aes(x = displ) ) + 
  geom_histogram() + 
  facet_wrap(~drv)

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.













ggplot(data = mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  facet_grid(drv ~ .)







ggplot(data = mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  facet_wrap(~drv, nrow = 3)

title: The layered grammar of graphics
text:
Template
ggplot(data = <DATA>) +
  <GEOM_FUNCTION>(
    mapping = aes(<MAPPINGS>),
    stat = <STAT>,
    position = <POSITION>
  ) +
  <COORDINATE_FUNCTION> +
  <FACET_FUNCTION>






Important



The seven parameters in the template compose the grammar of graphics, a formal system for building plots. The grammar of graphics is based on the insight that you can uniquely describe any plot as a combination of a dataset, a geom, a set of mappings, a stat, a position adjustment, a coordinate system, a faceting scheme, and a theme.


Two very useful resources for getting an overview of the complete ggplot2 functionality are the ggplot2 cheatsheet (which can be found at https://posit.co/resources/cheatsheets) and the ggplot2 package website (https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org).

title: communication
text:
Goal: Communicating understandings to others
\(\rightarrow\) make plots as self-explanatory as possible

title: communication
text:
Scales
Scales control how aesthetic mappings will be displayed

Default scales
ggplot2 automatically adds scales:

ggplot(
  data = mpg, 
  mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)
) +
  geom_point(
    mapping = aes(color = class)
  )







# Behind the scenes
ggplot(
  data = mpg,
  mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)
) + 
  geom_point(
    mapping = aes(color = class)
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous() + # puts values of displ on continuous line on x-axis
  scale_y_continuous() +
  scale_color_discrete() # chooses colors for each of the class of car








Scales are named according to type of variable:

continuous
discrete
datetime
date

Reasons to override defaults:

changing paramters of default scale, allowing e.g. to change breaks on axes, or key labels on the legend
replacing scales, and using different algorithm



Axis ticks and legend keys
Axis and legends are calles guides. Axis are used for x/y aesthetics; legends for everything else.
Two arguments affecting appearance of ticks on axis and keys on legend: breaks and labels:

break: controls position of ticks; - values associated with keys
labels: controls text labels


ggplot(
  data = mpg,
  mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy, color = drv)
) + 
  geom_point() +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(from = 15, to = 40, by = 5))








Can use labels same way, or set NULL to suppress. For discrete scales for categorical variables, labels can be named list of existing level names and desired labels for them:

# Example: Rescaling and relabeling x-axis
ggplot(
  data = mpg,
  mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)
) + 
  geom_point() +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = seq(from = 2, to = 7, length.out = 3),
    labels = c("A", "B", "C"))







# Example: labels = NULL
ggplot(
  data = mpg,
  mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy, color = drv)
) + 
  geom_point() +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = NULL) +
  scale_color_discrete(labels = c(
    "4" = "4-wheel",
    "f" = "front",
    "r" = "rear"
  ))














Tip



labels coupled with labelling functions from scales package useful for formatting numbers as currency, percent, etc.



# Left
ggplot(
  data = diamonds,
  mapping = aes(x = price, y = cut)
) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.05) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = label_dollar())







# Right
ggplot(
  data = diamonds,
  mapping = aes(x = price, y = cut)
) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.05) + 
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = label_dollar(scale = 1/1000, # divide dollar values by 1000
                          suffix = "K"), # add suffix
    breaks = seq(from = 1000, to = 19000, by = 6000) # add custom breaks
  )








Another label function is label_percent():

ggplot(
  data = diamonds,
  mapping = aes(x = cut, fill = clarity)
) +
   geom_bar(position = "fill") + # position = "fill" return proportions at every x-position; most useful for geoms anchored to x-axis, like geom_bar()
   scale_y_continuous(
    labels = label_percent(),
    name = "Percentage"
   )








Another use of breaks when having few data points, wanting to highlight where observations occur:

# Presidential dataset
glimpse(presidential)

Rows: 12
Columns: 4
$ name  <chr> "Eisenhower", "Kennedy", "Johnson", "Nixon", "Ford", "Carter", "…
$ start <date> 1953-01-20, 1961-01-20, 1963-11-22, 1969-01-20, 1974-08-09, 197…
$ end   <date> 1961-01-20, 1963-11-22, 1969-01-20, 1974-08-09, 1977-01-20, 198…
$ party <chr> "Republican", "Democratic", "Democratic", "Republican", "Republi…

presidential |>
  mutate(id = 33 + row_number()) |> # add id corresponding to number of pres.
  ggplot(
    mapping = aes(x = start, y = id)
  ) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_segment(
    mapping = aes(xend = end, yend = id)
  ) +
  scale_x_date(name = NULL,
               breaks = presidential$start,
               date_labels = "'%y"
  )










Legend layout
Controls overall position of the legend using theme() setting - they control non-data parts of the plot. Theme setting legend.position controls where the legend is drawn:

base <- ggplot(
  data = mpg, 
  mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)
) + 
  geom_point(
    mapping = aes(color = class)
  )

base + theme(legend.position = "right") # default







base + theme(legend.position = "left")







base +
  theme(legend.position = "top") +
  guides(color = guide_legend(nrow = 3))







base +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  guides(color = guide_legend(nrow = 3))














Note



guides() along with guides_legend() or guide_colorbar() controls the display of individual legends. nrowcontrols number of rows the legend uses; override.aes overrides one of the aesthetics, e.g., making points bigger. Useful when used a low alphato display many points on a plot.



ggplot(
  data = mpg,
  mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)
) + 
  geom_point(
    mapping = aes(color = class)
  ) + 
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom") +
  guides(color = guide_legend(nrow = 3, override.aes = list(size= 4)))

`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'















Note



Argument in guides() matches name of aesthetic, just like in labs().




Replacing a scale
Two types of scales likely to switch out: continuous position scales and color scales.

# Example - log transforming to better see relationship
# Left
ggplot(
  data = diamonds,
  mapping = aes(x = carat, y = price)
) + 
  geom_bin2d()

`stat_bin2d()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.








# Right
# Note: scales are labelled with transformed values
ggplot(
  data = diamonds,
  mapping = aes(x = log10(carat), y = log10(price))
) + 
  geom_bin2d()

`stat_bin2d()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.








# Instead doing transformation in aesthetic mapping, transform with scales
# Note: Scales are labelled with original values
ggplot(
  data = diamonds,
  mapping = aes(x = carat, y = price)
) + 
  geom_bin2d() +
  scale_x_log10() +
  scale_y_log10()

`stat_bin2d()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.









Another scale frequently customized is color. Default categorical scale picks colors that are evenly spaced around the color wheel.
Useful alternative: ColorBrewer - tuned colors for color blindness.

ggplot(
  data = mpg,
  mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)
) + 
  geom_point(
    mapping = aes(color = drv)
  )







# For people with red-green color blindness: 
ggplot(
  data = mpg,
  mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)
) +
  geom_point(
    mapping = aes(color = drv, shape = drv)
  ) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") # See documentation for palettes














Tip



For ColorBrewer scales see online documentation at https://colorbrewer2.org/.


For predefined mapping, use scale_color_manual():

presidential |>
  mutate(id = 33 + row_number()) |>
  ggplot(
    mapping = aes(x = start, y = id, color = party)
  ) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_segment(
    mapping = aes(yend = id, xend = end)
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    name = "Name of president",
    breaks = seq(34, 45, by = 1),
    labels = presidential$name
    ) +
  scale_x_date(
    name = "Start of presidency",
    date_breaks = "4 years", # see documentation
    date_labels = "'%y"
    ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(Republican = "red", Democratic = "blue")
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "top"
    ) +
  labs(
    title = "Presidential terms",
     color = "Party"
  )














Tip



For continuous color, use scale_color_gradient() or scale_fill_gradient(). For diverging scale use scale_color_gradient2() - allows to give negative and positive values different color.


One very useful color scale - viridis color scales:

df <- tibble(
  x = rnorm(10000),
  y =  rnorm(10000)
)

ggplot(
  data = df,
  mapping = aes(x = x, y = y)
) +
  geom_hex() +
  coord_fixed() +
  labs(
    title = "Default, continous",
    x = NULL, 
    y = NULL
  )







ggplot(
  data = df, 
  mapping = aes(x = x, y = y)
) + 
  geom_hex() + 
  coord_fixed() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c() +
  labs(
    title = "Viridius, continuous",
    x = NULL, 
    y = NULL
  )







ggplot(
  data = df, 
  mapping = aes(x = x, y = y)
) + 
  geom_hex() +
  coord_fixed() +
  scale_fill_viridis_b() +
  labs(
    title = "Viridius, binned",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL
  )







ggplot(df, aes(x, y)) +
  geom_hex() +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "red") +
  coord_fixed()














Note



Color scales come in two variables: scale_color_*() and scale_fill_*().




Zooming
Three ways to control plot limits:. 1. Adjust what data are plotted. 2. Set limits in each scale. 3. Set xlim and ylim in coord_cartesian()

# Left
ggplot(
  data = mpg, 
  mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)
) +
  geom_point(
    mapping = aes(color = drv)
  ) +
  geom_smooth()

`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'








# Right
mpg |> 
  filter(displ >= 5 & displ <= 6 & hwy >= 10 & hwy <= 25) |>
  ggplot(
    mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)
  ) +
  geom_point(
    mapping = aes(color = drv)
  ) + 
  geom_smooth() + 
  scale_color_discrete(
    labels = c(
      "4" = "4-wheel",
      "f" = " front",
      "r" = "rear"
    )
  )

`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'








# compare 'Right' to: 
ggplot(
  data = mpg, 
  mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)
) +
  geom_point(
    mapping = aes(color = drv)
  ) +
  geom_smooth() +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(5, 6), ylim = c(10, 25))

`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'

title: communication
text:
Themes
Customizing non-data elements of the plot with a theme:

ggplot(
  data = mpg, 
  mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)
) +
  geom_point(
    mapping = aes(color = drv)
  ) +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE) +
  theme_bw()

`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'















Note



ggplot includes 12 themes: *_bw(), *_light(), *_classic(), *_linedraw(), *_dark(), *_minimal(), *_gray(), *_void(); Many more in add-on packages like ggthemes.



ggplot(
  data = mpg, 
  mapping = aes(x= displ, y = hwy, color = drv)
) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    title = "Larger engine sizes tend to have lower fuel economy",
    caption = "Source: https://fueleconomy.gov."
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position = c(0.6, 0.7), # position of legend in plot
    legend.direction = "horizontal", # layout of items in legend
    legend.box.background = element_rect(color = "red"),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 1),
    plot.title.position = "plot",
    plot.caption.position = "plot",
    plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = "blue")
  )

title: communication
text:
Layout
Patchwork package allows combining separate plots into same graphic:

p1 <- ggplot(mpg, aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(title = "Plot 1")
p2 <- ggplot(mpg, aes(x = drv, y = hwy)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(title = "Plot 2")

p1 + p2 # First create and save both plots, then place next to each other with +







# Or more complex plot layout:
p3 <- ggplot(mpg, aes(x = cty, y = hwy)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(title = "Plot 3")

(p1 | p3) / p2








Patchwork allows collecting legends from multiple plots into one common legend - customizing placement, dimension, add title, subtitle, caption, etc. to the plots \(\rightarrow\) look through the guides on the package website: https://patchwork.data-imaginist.com.
